speaker: interviewer
text: 1995. We were talking about the wealthy young man--

speaker: interviewee
text: The wealthy young man, yeah. Now, he really was from the underground. There were different undergrounds in Belgium. There was a Catholic underground, which was nationalist, nationalist underground, patriotic Belgian patriots. That's where he belonged. There was also a Communist underground. There was a very tiny Jewish underground, also. Very tiny. They-- he provided us a woman, when, in fact, she was related to the other woman, and that woman, also, for money. But she was willing to rent a house and to occupy the ground floor of the house, do all the shopping for us, and we would occupy the rest of the house. It was also in a non-Jewish neighborhood. It was a five-minute walk from the Gestapo headquarters. And we figured that's a good place to hide because they say, where the dog lives, he doesn't bite. But it didn't turn out that way, but that's what we figured. Anyway, they-- they rented that house, and we moved in. And at that time, moved into this house, moved in my parents, my brother, myself. My sister, that was an [? assessment ?] that we're hiding in that place where all the Dutch Jews were. And also another brother-in-law with his wife and his child remained in hiding where they were, but he came to us since we had the whole house, but it was a tiny house. And we occupied the first, second, third floor. The ground floor, the woman occupied, and she also had the basement. She was alone. She had no husband. And that was our hiding place. Life in-- in the hiding place was not easy. We never went out in the street. The streets were very dangerous and becoming more dangerous all the time because, as all the Jews that volunteered to come to-- to Mechelen, to camp, that received these subpoenas to come, when that ended, the only Jews they could find is to look for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had cars in the city. The cars had a-- you could recognize, the cars could be recognized that they Gestapo cars because passed the number, it had the letters POL, Polizei. That was Gestapo. So they were roaming around the streets to look for Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was also in Brussels, the Jew, an informant, which was called, we called him [? Jack ?] the Informer. In Yiddish, they called him [? Jack ?] the [YIDDISH]. [YIDDISH] means an informer. He-- he was a crazy-- he must've been crazy because his entire family was deported. But he-- he had a talent to recognize Jews. So he was with them. He was driving around with them. He would recognize Jews, stop them in the street and arrest them. And take them away. I don't know the number, but thousands of Jews were discovered in hiding places. Little by little, they found every hiding place. It wasn't easy to hide. It became tighter and tighter. The fewer there were left, the more dangerous it became because they didn't have that much jobs, that much work to do. And there were still the same amount of Gestapos. Because same way they used the Judenrat, the Jews to facilitate their task by giving them all the addresses of all the Jews, the same way they used the countries where they occupied. They used the police or they used the local SS that they engaged. They formed the Belgian SS, and they dressed in black uniforms. Usually it was the lowest of the lowest-- ex-convicts, or the real garbage of the people that joined the SS, people that could never achieve anything. They felt proud. They gave them a uniform. They gave them a gun. And they gave them people to rule over. I mean, these people were nothings.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Belgian SS facilitated their task. They did the dirty work for the Germans. The Germans were the supervisors. The Belgian SS did the dirty work-- the arresting and the beating, and they-- they were the low end. So the-- the Gestapo consisted in Brussels of Germans in uniform and in civilian clothes, and of Belgian SS which helped them. That informant, that Jack was responsible for hundreds, or maybe thousands of Jews that he-- that he spotted in the streets. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But that's beside the point.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, life in-- in the hiding place was difficult. It-- it could be boring. And I, as a boy, I figured, I have to have a plan. Otherwise we can get-- we get crazy. I had a-- my plan consisted-- I was studying English. I was studying Dutch. We didn't have any Hebrew books. We couldn't have, but I did have one Bible. So I was studying the prophet Isaiah by heart. Every day a little piece. There's 66 chapters in Isaiah. I was long enough in the hiding place to study the entire Isaiah by heart and a few other books, Proverbs and some other prophets. So that took up my time. Also, this young man that I mentioned before, Piere Carnavelle, he used to come and pay us visits. One of the reasons why he paid us visits is that he himself didn't want to walk about the streets, even saw that he was not Jewish. But he was from the underground, and he was really active in the underground. And he, once, not once but several times, he brought a machine gun to the house, and he said, I have to stay to the evening. I have a job in the evening. And in the evening, they would go out and put away somebody that was-- that was real bad, an informer or a-- or a Belgian that would, that worked close with the Gestapo, that would find Jews and underground people. That was their business. He really was a hero. And he had a friend. He-- he was a-- a law student. All these boys were from aristocratic families. So he used to come. He used to play chess with us. And he used to spend many, many days with us, the entire day. And the evening, he would go out to do his job, whatever it was. And time-- time went by. We were in hiding from August '42 until we were caught on April 27, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how they found you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a hint, but not exactly. While we were in hiding, after-- after a while, it was very difficult to take. And this young man, this Piere Carnavelle said, you know, during the summer, I'm going to take you out on Sundays to the forest. There was less Gestapo activity on Sunday. And that's what he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a few Sundays, he took my brother and my sister and my brother-in-law and myself. We went out to the forest, and we spent a day in the fresh air. And so that helped a little to break up the monotony of being in hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: We also didn't have any fresh air year-round. What I did is, on the-- the top floor of the house, you know the roofs are-- are slanted in the old houses in Europe, they're slanted roofs. And they called that room, [NON-ENGLISH]. A roof with a slanted-- a room with a slanted ceiling they called a [NON-ENGLISH]. You could-- there was a window in the ceiling. In the slanted ceiling was a window. You could push up that window, and I would go on a chair, and stand on that chair, and stick my head out on the roof. That's how I got fresh air.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was going down for-- for quite awhile until 1944, that type of life. And at the end, it became very difficult to stay-- to stay in hiding. We also, we didn't have proper food. We didn't have meat for two years. The only thing we had, we had herring. They gave herring.

speaker: interviewee
text: You need to ration-- you needed ration-- rationing cards for any food. That was provided to us by the underground on my name, on my fake name, [? June ?] [? Murrow. ?] [? June ?] [? Murrow ?] also had a-- a food card, a rationing card. And so the-- the woman that was taking care of us could take our cards and get fish, get herring in the stores. But it, we had enough food to survive. We-- we could have survived even a longer time. But you didn't, we didn't have any more at the end, this money. The money was going towards the end. Money, actually, the inflation was tremendous. So we didn't have any money. We kept everything in gold because gold was going up in value every day. And so were the prices of food. But it's thanks to the gold that we could match the food prices.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what form of gold? Was it coins?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not coins, it was pieces. You know, how you call these--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bars?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not bars. You know, old gold, old pieces, broken-- broken-- broken pieces that we had bought, and we kept it. That was a problem, how to sell it. How can you go out and sell it? So we had a Belgian that we knew from-- from before going into hiding, and who appears to be a nice person. And we sent the woman to him every month to sell a few pieces of gold. And that's how we had enough money to pay her and to live there. We had to pay rent for the house. We had to pay for her. And we had to pay for the food, which was quite expensive. Whatever was not rationed was very expensive, like butter or normal bread. The bread that they gave you, you could hardly eat. It was like cement. So we had to buy bread on the black market, which was very expensive. Anyway, we could have managed. And we stayed in that hiding place for all that time until my brother got sick. He couldn't take it anymore. First, they took away his wife in the raid on the train a few months after he married, never saw her again. He loved her very much. And then the hiding had a very bad reaction on him, and he had a nervous breakdown. He couldn't take it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened at that time, the-- they had deported already at that time, I don't know how many thousands. They deported about 25,000 Jews from my Holland. Only a handful came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: By that time, they might have deported 20,000 or 18,000, and-- which was something which was different in Belgium from all the other countries. You know, they had, like I said, Mechelen was a [NON-ENGLISH], it was a place where they assembled the Jews to be deported. They had a place like this in Holland, which was called Westerbork. And they had a place like this in France which was called [INAUDIBLE]. Now, in Belgium, they took, sometimes Jews that were arrested with children, they would take from the basement in the Gestapo. The-- when Jews were arrested, they were taken to the basement of that Gestapo headquarters that I mentioned before. And they stayed there in the basement, and I'll come back to that when I was arrested. And sometimes they would send from the Judenrat, a girl that would ask the parents if they want the children to be in a home, in a children's home. And the parents that agreed, would give the child to this woman, and they placed-- the Judenrat was surrounding several children's home-- homes in-- around Brussels. I don't know exactly. I know for sure there were three, maybe there were five.

speaker: interviewer
text: In each home, there were a few hundred children, and that was official. The Germans knew about these homes. And they tolerated these homes. Why they did it? Nobody knew. There was suppositions.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there were times where Jews were caught, and they would be sent away with the children to Auschwitz. And, as you know, children-- women with children-- were killed immediately. And there were times that they would give Jewish mothers the alternative to let the children be taken away by the Judenrat and placed into homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a number of Jewish children in these homes. These homes were run by the Judenrat, and they were employees in these homes, Jewish young men and girls. These people that worked in the homes had a so-called protection of being deported as long as they worked there.

speaker: interviewee
text: The idea why they did it is, some people, there was a rumor, that the queen, that the mother queen, Belgium was a kingdom, the king of Belgium at that time was a collaborator. After the war, he was banished from Belgium. But his mother was trying to help Jews. And apparently, it was through her intervention that they established these few homes for some of the children. Now, whoever was lucky enough and whichever mother had the foresight to give the child to them, they saved, that child would have been saved. They also had, even saw that they-- they deported thousands of elderly Jews from Belgium. They made an old age home. And they would take, there were no rules by them. One day they would do this, and the next day they would do something different. And sometimes when they caught elderly Jews, they would send them to the old age home, which at the end of the war, had a few hundred elderly Jews. What we figured out, the reason why they did this, is because the Gestapo people that were handling the Jewish section had it very good in Belgium. They had a type of life that they couldn't have in Germany. They lived in luxury. And they wanted to extend for themselves just as long as possible. So they tried to drag out these things with the Jews, and this way they still had a few hundred Jews in reserve. And they could claim in-- in the headquarters in Berlin that there's still Jews in Belgium. That's what we figured out. We couldn't figure out any other reason. The-- so my brother, and I'll come back, he couldn't-- couldn't take it anymore. And he went to a doctor. And the doctor told him that he needs, he has to have an occupation, or it's not gonna be good with him. He-- he decided to try to get a job in the Jewish old age home. And he did get a job in the Jewish old age home, but it was, it-- the risk to us became very, very great because he was moving out from the hiding place every day to the Jewish old age home. And my supposition is that one day he was followed, and that's how they found our hiding place. Because it happened a short while after he started to work in that old age home. Now, what I have to come back is that while we were hiding-- in the beginning, when we were hiding, my brother and myself, we were always very active in the religious Zionist movement. And in every town in Europe, there was a committee. The committee consisted of a delegate from each Zionist party. There were many Jewish Zionist parties. There was the left and the right and the middle. Each one had a delegate in that committee. That committee was called the Palestinian Office. The reason it was called Palestinian Office because the only work that committee was doing is, as you know, the British had the mandate Palestine. And the-- they gave immigration certificates to Jews every few months. There was a quota of immigration certificates, which were called certificates. The only job of that committee was to divide the certificates.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's say, Brussels received 20 certificates. So that had to be divided amongst the different political Zionist parties. Let's say two went to the religious, two went to the leftist, two went to the liberals, and so forth. That was the purpose of that Palestinian Office, which was a committee. And that Palestinian Office you had in every town in Europe where there was a Jewish community because certificates were coming to every town. And the certificates had to be decided, who is getting the certificates. So my brother, my older brother, was the delegate of the Mizrahi movement, which was the religious Zionist aided in that Palestinian Office. Now, when we went into hiding, of course, there were no more Jewish activities whatsoever, except the Judenrat because everything was against the law. You were not allowed to have a synagogue, or a-- or a Jewish movement, or Jews get together. Everything had to be liquidated. There was no Jewish activities except the Judenrat, which was controlled by the Gestapo. So when we were in hiding in the beginning in 1942, maybe it was in September and October, my brother somehow got word that the Palestinian Office is-- has a clandestine meeting somewhere, and that he should come. It's important. We-- we did not want him to go because we figured that he's exposing himself to danger, and it could be a set up thing. There were many set up things like that. And then, when people would get together, the Gestapo would break in and take them away. So we thought it could be a set up, or it's unimportant. But he insisted he wants to go. He said maybe there's some certificates, maybe came in. Maybe we'll be able to get out of here or something. He insisted, and he went.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that meeting, which was completely unimportant to us at the time, a man appeared at that meeting. His name was [? Neikirk ?]. He was a Belgian Jew who had a Belgian name, and he was a Zionist. He had fake papers as a non-Jew, very courageous man. I never met him, but my brother did. He was a courier. He came from Switzerland. He braved the Gestapo, the borders, and the checks, and everything. They didn't recognize him. And he came up with the news that there are exchange certificates being prepared. That there is a number of Germans in Palestine, and these civilian. And these Germans were interned by the British as enemy subjects. And the International Red Cross wants to exchange these few thousand Germans from Palestine for British subjects in-- that are stuck in Europe and that were interned by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Jewish agency-- of course, there was no Israel at that time, there was, you know, [? is ?] no government. The Jewish agency was the Jewish, was the leadership. The Jewish agency insisted [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so much with the British that Jews, Palestinian Jews, that were stuck in Europe should be added to the exchange list for the Palestinian Germans.
